1: Asa did what was good and right in the eyes of YHWH his God — wayyaʿaś ʾAsāʾ haṭṭôḇ wᵉhayyāšār bᵉʿênê YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw. The double evaluation formula (“good and right”) appears in Deuteronomy as the ideal covenantal obedience (Deut 6:18; 12:28) and in Chronicles for the best kings (2 Chr 31:20; Hezekiah). Asa’s reign (910-869 BC, 41 years) is the longest of the good-king reigns in Chronicles.
2: Asa removes the foreign altars and the high places, breaks down the pillars (massēḇôt), cuts down the Asherim. The threefold destruction — altars, pillars, Asherim — targets the three most common forms of Canaanite cult infrastructure. Massēḇôt (standing stones/pillars) could be legitimate YHWH worship markers in early Israel (Gen 28:18; Exod 24:4) but became associated with Baalism and were prohibited (Deut 16:22); Asa breaks them. ʾĂšērîm (sacred poles or trees associated with the goddess Asherah) are cut down throughout the reform narratives.
3: Asa commanded Judah to seek YHWH (liḏrôš ʾet-YHWH) and to keep the law and the commandment. The command to seek YHWH is the Chronicler’s foundational covenantal instruction — the same dāraš that will characterize every good king and whose absence characterizes every failed king. The conjunction of seeking YHWH and keeping Torah is the Chronicler’s version of the Shema’s call: love YHWH and keep his commands.
4: He also removed the high places and the incense altars from all the cities of Judah — habbāmôt wᵉhammᵉḥammānîm. The mᵉḥammānîm (incense altars or sun-pillars) are a distinctly Chronicler-preferred term (also in 2 Chr 34:4, 7; Lev 26:30; Isa 17:8; 27:9) for a type of illicit cult installation. And the kingdom had rest under him. The causal link — reform followed by rest — is the Chronicler’s thesis statement for the reign: obedience produces šeqeṭ (quietness/rest).
5: Wayyiḇen ʿārê mibṣār bîhûḏāh kî šāqᵉṭāh hāʾāreṣ wᵉʾên-mᵉlāmāh bašššānîm hāhēm kî-hēnîaḥ YHWH lô — He built fortified cities in Judah while the land was quiet and there was no war against him, for the LORD gave him peace (hēnîaḥ lô). The building of fortifications during peace is strategic preparation from a position of strength — using the gift of rest to create infrastructure for future defense. The hēnîaḥ YHWH lô (YHWH gave him rest) explicitly attributes the peace to divine gift, not military achievement.
6: Asa says to Judah: “Let us build these cities and surround them with walls and towers, gates and bars. The land is still ours because we have sought the LORD our God (dāraštānû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû); we have sought him, and he has given us peace on every side.” The king’s speech connects building activity to the prior act of dāraš — the infrastructure of defense is understood as the fruit of covenant faithfulness, not the cause of security. Security comes from YHWH; buildings are its expression.
7: So they built and prospered (wayyiḇnû wayyaṣlîḥû). Asa’s army: 300,000 from Judah bearing large shields and spears (ṣinnāh wᵉrōmaḥ); 280,000 from Benjamin bearing small shields and bows. The tribal breakdown — Judah with heavy infantry weapons (large shield + spear), Benjamin with ranged weapons (small shield + bow) — reflects historical tribal military specializations. The total of 580,000 is Judah’s fighting force at the height of Asa’s early reign.
8: Zerah the Cushite comes out against them with an army of 1,000,000 (ʾelep ʾălāpîm — a thousand thousands) and 300 chariots and comes to Mareshah. Zerah is identified as a Cushite (Kûšî) — possibly an Egyptian commander of Nubian (Cushite) troops, or an otherwise unknown African general. The ratio of 580,000 (Israel) to 1,000,000 (Cushite) is nearly 2:1 against Judah, establishing the holy-war context: victory will require divine rather than human military superiority.
9: Asa went out against him — in the Valley of Zephathah at Mareshah. Mareshah is in the Shephelah (foothills) southwest of Jerusalem — strategically significant as the entry point to the Judahite heartland from the southwest. To lose at Mareshah would expose Jerusalem itself. Asa’s decision to meet the Cushites in the valley rather than retreat to defended cities reflects either military confidence or the holy-war posture of advancing with YHWH.
10: Asa cries to YHWH his God: YHWH ʾên-ʿimmᵉḵā laʿăzōr bên-rāḇ lᵉʾên kōaḥ ʿăzrēnû YHWH ʾĕlōhênû kî-ʿāleḵā nišʿānnû ûḇᵉšimḵā-bānû ʿal-hehāmôn hazzeh YHWH ʾĕlōhênû ʾattāh ʾal-yaʿăṣōr ʿimmᵉḵā ʾĕnôš — “O LORD, there is none like you to help, between the mighty and the weak. Help us, O LORD our God, for we rely on you, and in your name we have come against this multitude. O LORD, you are our God; let not man prevail against you.” The prayer names four elements: divine incomparability, divine help available to both strong and weak, human trust in the divine name, and the framing of the battle as YHWH vs. man (not Israel vs. Cushites).
11: YHWH defeats the Cushites before Asa and before Judah, and the Cushites flee. Wayyiggōp YHWH ʾet-hakḵûšîm — the LORD struck down the Cushites. The divine agent of defeat is explicit: this is not Asa’s tactical brilliance but YHWH’s direct intervention. The battle is narrated in a single verse — because the narrative is not really about the battle but about what preceded it (the prayer) and follows it (the reform).
12: Asa and the people with him pursue them as far as Gerar — ʿaḏ-Gerār — and the Cushites fall until none survive, for they were broken before YHWH and his army (liḇnê YHWH wᵉlaḥayyālô). The victorious army is described as “YHWH’s army” — the designation given to Joshua (Josh 5:14-15) and to Israel in the Exodus (Exod 12:41). The human army is YHWH’s army because it is fighting in YHWH’s name and by YHWH’s power.
13: They strike down all the cities around Gerar — the Philistine-adjacent territory — for the fear of YHWH comes upon them. The regional ripple effect of YHWH’s victory on behalf of Judah — surrounding territories falling to the terror of YHWH (paḥaḏ YHWH) — is the same phenomenon described in the conquest narratives (Josh 2:9; 10:2) and in Jehoshaphat’s era (2 Chr 17:10; 20:29). YHWH’s visible intervention creates a sphere of divine fear that extends beyond the battlefield.
14: They plunder all the cities (for there was much plunder in them) and they strike down the tents of cattle-keepers and carry away sheep in abundance and camels. The spoil of the Cushite campaign is agricultural wealth (flocks and herds) — indicating the campaign reaches into the pastoral hinterlands of Gerar. The abundance of spoil is the Chronicler’s sign of divine blessing: the one who fights for YHWH is enriched by YHWH.
15: Then Asa and all Judah and Benjamin return to Jerusalem. The return to Jerusalem — the covenantal center — closes the campaign narrative. The circuit is complete: the people leave Jerusalem trusting YHWH, fight in his name, win by his power, and return to the city of YHWH’s dwelling. The geographical movement embodies the theological movement of the Chronicler’s ideal: seek YHWH → go out → win → return and give thanks.
